wall_line_handles.reason: full-width line at y=0, likely wall face of opening
full-width line at y=200, likely opposite wall face

notes: 1200-wide window block (phg) on WIN layer: parallel glazing lines between two full-span wall-face lines.

rationale.evidence: All 22 entities sit on a dedicated WIN layer and the name 'phg1200' matches the ~1160mm bbox width. Long parallel horizontal lines at y=0, 40, 60, 100, 200 spanning 40..1160 form the classic plan-view window sandwich (frame/glass lines between wall faces), with small LWPOLYLINEs as mullion/frame details.
rationale.rule: A compact block whose entities all live on a WIN/DOOR-type layer and consist of a few long parallel lines bounded by two outer face lines is an opening symbol, not building fabric itself; its outer face lines coincide with wall faces.
rationale.counterfactual: If the layer histogram showed WAL-type layers or hatched poche polylines instead of WIN, or the bbox depth exceeded typical wall thickness (~200mm here), I would reclassify toward 평면 부분도 and raise wall_likelihood.
rationale.uncertainty: Without linetype/color or the parent-plan insertion context I cannot confirm whether y=0/y=200 lines are literal wall faces or frame outlines; LWPOLYLINE vertex coordinates are not shown, so their shapes are inferred.

wall_line_handles.reason: long vertical line paired 30mm from 4118, possible wall/frame stub
parallel mate of 4117, possible wall/frame stub

notes: 750-wide door unit block (pkdr750190) on DOOR layer: leaf, frame and side-panel geometry.

rationale.evidence: All 25 entities are on a single DOOR layer and the name encodes a door size (750/190). Vertical line groups at x=0,-10,-20,-50 spanning y=750..1420 read as a door leaf/side panel in plan, and the diagonal ticks (4128, 412A) are typical frame chamfer marks; many small LWPOLYLINEs are frame sections.
rationale.rule: Blocks confined to a DOOR layer with size-encoded names and leaf/frame line bundles are opening symbols; parallel thin line pairs inside them are frame or leaf edges rather than structural walls.
rationale.counterfactual: If the paired lines 4117/4118 were much thicker apart (100mm+) and extended beyond the door extents, or a WAL-type layer appeared, I would treat them as wall stubs and raise wall_likelihood.
rationale.uncertainty: LWPOLYLINE vertex data is hidden, so I cannot verify whether any polyline traces a wall stub; the 30mm pair spacing is ambiguous between a frame member and a thin partition edge.

wall_line_handles.reason: long vertical line paired 30mm from 6CCD, possible wall/frame stub
parallel mate of 6CCC, possible wall/frame stub

notes: Variant copy of the pkdr750190 door block (taller side panel to y=1460), same DOOR-layer composition.

rationale.evidence: Entity mix (8 LINE, 17 LWPOLYLINE on one DOOR layer) and coordinates are near-identical to defannot-x-0-pkdr750190-362, differing only in the panel top (1460 vs 1420). The '-1' suffix marks an anonymous-style duplicate created when a block is redefined or copied with edits.
rationale.rule: When a block duplicates an already-classified sibling with only a dimensional tweak and a numeric suffix, it inherits the sibling's role; suffix duplicates of opening symbols remain opening symbols.
rationale.counterfactual: If the duplicate had gained WAL-layer entities or lost the leaf/frame bundle, the inheritance would break and it would need independent classification.
rationale.uncertainty: As with its sibling, hidden LWPOLYLINE vertices prevent confirming frame vs wall-stub geometry, and the actual functional difference between the two variants (door height vs adjacent panel) is not determinable from this projection.

notes: Small 5-entity PS-door 700 block on STL layer: swing arc plus diagonal leaf line and jamb polylines.

rationale.evidence: Only 5 entities: one ARC (door swing), one long diagonal LINE (-330,30)->(243.8,361.3) that reads as an open leaf, and three 4-vertex LWPOLYLINEs that read as jamb/frame rectangles. Name PSD700 encodes a 700-size pipe-shaft or PS door.
rationale.rule: An arc plus a single leaf line plus a couple of jamb rectangles is the minimal plan-door symbol signature regardless of layer name; entity economy (<10) also argues for a symbol over a plan fragment.
rationale.counterfactual: If the polylines were long and parallel spanning beyond the swing extents, they could be wall stubs and the block might carry wall geometry; the ARC being a full circle instead of a swing would suggest a different fixture.
rationale.uncertainty: ARC parameters (center/radius) are not shown, so the swing interpretation rests on the name and the diagonal line; the STL layer's meaning (steel? shaft?) cannot be verified from this projection.

notes: 1312-entity spline-heavy block on FUR layer, 1500x830 footprint: detailed furniture/appliance (likely sink-bench or sofa-bed unit) plan.

rationale.evidence: Every one of the 1312 entities is on the FUR layer, with 814 SPLINEs and 164 ARCs — organic curve density typical of manufacturer-drawn furniture/fixture blocks. The 1500x830 bbox matches a catalog furniture module and the name 'SB1703 1500' looks like a product code plus width.
rationale.rule: Blocks living entirely on a FUR layer with very high spline/arc counts and a product-code name are imported furniture symbols; walls are never drawn with hundreds of splines.
rationale.counterfactual: If a second layer such as WAL appeared, or long straight lines traced the bbox perimeter at wall-like 100-200mm pair offsets, I would suspect embedded room fabric and raise wall_likelihood.
rationale.uncertainty: The exact object type (sink bench, sofa, appliance) cannot be resolved from 30 sampled entities out of 1312; sampling may also hide a few non-FUR strays beyond what the layer histogram shows it cannot.

notes: Stair plan fragment on STA layer: tread lines every 260mm with break-line gaps at x=-1250..-1400, plus NON/SYM annotation entities.

rationale.evidence: 34 LINEs on the STA layer form evenly spaced tread lines (y=-1300,-1820,-2080,... step 260) split into left/right runs with short 25mm stubs around x=-1250..-1350 — a classic stair-with-break-line plan. Multiple layers (STA, NON, SYM, DEFPOINTS-1) plus CIRCLEs indicate an assembled drawing fragment, not a single-purpose symbol.
rationale.rule: A block mixing building-element layers with annotation/symbol layers and depicting an area of the building (stair flight with break lines) is a partial plan fragment rather than a reusable unit symbol.
rationale.counterfactual: If all entities sat on one SYM-type layer or the geometry were a bare arrow/tag, I would call it 심볼; if wall-thickness line pairs bounded the stair shaft, wall_likelihood would rise sharply.
rationale.uncertainty: The NON-layer polylines' shapes are hidden, so I cannot rule out that one traces the stair-shaft walls; CIRCLE radii and SYM-layer content (likely a direction arrow) are also not visible.

notes: Variant of the sta-t stair fragment extended one tread deeper (to y=-3380), same STA/NON/SYM layer mix.

rationale.evidence: Entity composition (38 LINE on STA, NON=20, SYM=7, 2 CIRCLE) and tread coordinates replicate sta-t exactly through y=-2860, with the bottom tread moved to -3380 — a longer flight. The '-4' suffix marks a copied/redefined variant of the same stair fragment.
rationale.rule: Suffix variants that preserve a sibling's layer mix and geometry pattern inherit its classification; changing flight length does not change what the block is.
rationale.counterfactual: If the variant had dropped the STA tread pattern or collapsed to a single symbol layer, inheritance would not apply and I would reclassify.
rationale.uncertainty: Same blind spots as sta-t: hidden NON/SYM polyline shapes could conceal shaft-wall outlines, and the functional difference between variants (which floor/flight) is not recoverable here.

notes: Third sta-t stair-fragment variant (40 STA tread lines, bbox to y=0, fewer SYM entities), same break-line tread pattern.

rationale.evidence: 40 STA-layer LINEs repeat the identical tread/break-line pattern of sta-t and sta-t-4 (rows at -1300..-3380 with 25mm stubs near x=-1300), with NON=21 and SYM reduced to 3. The bbox extends to y=0, suggesting extra landing geometry beyond the sampled rows.
rationale.rule: Same inheritance rule as other suffix variants: preserved layer mix plus preserved geometry pattern means preserved role (stair plan fragment).
rationale.counterfactual: If the additional entities near y=0 turned out to be wall poche or shaft outline pairs, wall_likelihood would rise; if SYM/NON vanished entirely, a pure 심볼 reading would become viable.
rationale.uncertainty: The sample truncates at 30 of 65 entities, so the landing-area geometry implied by the y=0 bbox edge is unseen; NON-layer polyline shapes remain unverifiable.

notes: Narrow 90x670 FUR-layer block of evenly spaced 20mm rungs — reads as a ladder/towel-rail/shelf unit in plan or elevation.

rationale.evidence: All 33 entities are on the FUR layer; 24+ LINEs run horizontally 10..90 at a constant 20mm pitch over a 90-wide, 670-tall strip, framed by LWPOLYLINEs — a rung/slat pattern typical of a ladder, towel rail, or slatted shelf fixture. The terse name 'T1' is a numbered fixture tag.
rationale.rule: Single-FUR-layer blocks with small footprints and regular slat patterns are furniture/fixture symbols; regular sub-25mm line pitch is joinery detail, never wall structure.
rationale.counterfactual: If the strip were 100-200mm wide with only two boundary lines, it could pass for a wall segment; the presence of a dense rung fill rules that out.
rationale.uncertainty: Whether this is drawn in plan (grating/slats seen from above) or is a mixed elevation fragment cannot be determined; T1's meaning in the project legend is unknown from this projection.

notes: Wood door 900 block (wd120900) on DOOR layer: swing arc, leaf line, frame polylines, and a long threshold line pair at y=80/77.

rationale.evidence: 16 entities all on DOOR layer with one ARC (swing), a short diagonal LINE (-20,120)->(0,0) as the leaf, high-vertex LWPOLYLINEs (17/19 vertices) as frame sections, and a 3mm-spaced long pair (1D17/1D18) spanning 40..860 that reads as a threshold/sill line. Name encodes wood door, 120 frame, 900 width.
rationale.rule: DOOR-layer blocks with size-coded 'wd' names, one swing arc, and frame polylines are door symbols; long thin pairs at 3mm offset are threshold lines, not walls (walls pair at 100-200mm).
rationale.counterfactual: If the long pair were offset by wall-scale distance or extended past the door bbox, I would flag them as wall stubs and raise wall_likelihood.
rationale.uncertainty: ARC geometry and polyline vertices are hidden, so leaf/frame reading rests on the name convention; whether the block includes any jamb-embedded wall return cannot be confirmed.

notes: Wood door 750 block on DOOR layer with X-crossed jamb marks at both ends and a swing arc.

rationale.evidence: 15 DOOR-layer entities; the two line pairs (1D21/1D22 at x=727..750 and 1D26/1D27 at x=0..23) form the X-cross jamb symbols conventional at door frames, plus one ARC for the swing and frame LWPOLYLINEs. Name wd160750 encodes a 750-wide wood door with 160 frame depth, matching the 750x140 bbox.
rationale.rule: X-crossed short line pairs at both ends of a size-coded DOOR-layer block are frame-section marks — a strong door-symbol signature; nothing wall-like remains once frame and swing are accounted for.
rationale.counterfactual: If the crosses were absent and instead two long parallel lines ran the full width at frame depth, a lintel/wall-over reading would compete and raise wall_likelihood.
rationale.uncertainty: The ARC's extent is unknown, so the swing direction/angle is assumed; hidden polyline shapes could include a sill line I cannot see.

notes: Wood door 750 block, 170 frame depth — near-identical twin of wd160750 with 150 bbox height.

rationale.evidence: Identical histogram to wd160750 (ARC=1, LINE=4, LWPOLYLINE=10, all DOOR layer) and identical X-cross jamb pairs (6921/6922, 6926/6927) at the same x positions, differing only in height (150 vs 140) per the deeper 170 frame in the name.
rationale.rule: A block that replicates an already-classified door block's structure with only a frame-depth change is the same symbol family; size-family members share one role.
rationale.counterfactual: Any added layer or loss of the swing arc plus jamb crosses would break the family inheritance and force independent judgment.
rationale.uncertainty: Same as its twin: arc extent and polyline vertices unseen, so internal frame details are assumed from convention rather than observed.

notes: Wood door 900 block with 200 frame depth — same template as wd120900 with threshold pair at y=160/157.

rationale.evidence: 16 DOOR-layer entities exactly mirroring wd120900's composition: leaf line (-20,200)->(0,0), swing ARC, the same 17/19-vertex frame polylines, and a 3mm threshold pair (410F/4110) spanning 40..860. Only the frame depth (200 vs 120) differs, as the name encodes.
rationale.rule: Template-stamped door blocks that vary only the size digits in the name and one dimension in geometry belong to one symbol family and share the door-symbol role.
rationale.counterfactual: A wall-scale offset in the long line pair or extra non-DOOR layers would be the flip trigger toward wall-bearing geometry.
rationale.uncertainty: Cannot verify from the projection whether the 200 depth is frame or includes a wall-return; arc parameters are hidden.

notes: Wood door 900 block with 250 frame depth — same door-symbol template, threshold pair at y=210/207.

rationale.evidence: Composition matches the wd-900 family (16 entities, DOOR layer, leaf line (-20,250)->(0,0), one ARC, 17/19-vertex frame polylines, 3mm threshold pair 3842/3843 spanning 40..860); the name's 250 matches the bbox height.
rationale.rule: Same size-family inheritance rule as WD200900/wd120900: template geometry plus size-coded name equals one shared door-symbol role.
rationale.counterfactual: Departure from the template (missing swing arc, added hatch poche, non-DOOR layers) would invalidate inheritance and could indicate embedded wall fabric.
rationale.uncertainty: As across the family, hidden ARC and polyline internals mean the frame-vs-wall boundary at the jambs is assumed from naming convention, not measured.

notes: 850-wide door block on DOOR layer drawn leftward from origin: frame polylines plus three near-collinear threshold lines, no swing arc sampled.

rationale.evidence: All 12 entities on DOOR layer; the same 17/19-vertex frame LWPOLYLINE signature seen in the wd-900 family, plus three long lines (584C/584D/584E) at y=0 and y=-3 spanning -30..-820 that read as threshold/sill lines for an 850 opening (name WD850).
rationale.rule: Frame-polyline signature plus size-coded WD name on a DOOR layer identifies a door symbol even when the swing arc is absent (sliding or arc-less representation); 3mm line offsets are graphic, not structural.
rationale.counterfactual: If the three long lines were spaced at wall thickness rather than 3mm, or a WAL layer appeared, I would treat this as an opening cut into wall fabric and raise wall_likelihood.
rationale.uncertainty: No ARC exists in the histogram, so the door type (sliding vs swing, or a frame-only lintel block) cannot be pinned down; the near-duplicate lines 584C/584D suggest drafting duplication I cannot confirm.

notes: Flagpole-holder (국기계양대) detail symbol on layer 0: 64x140 bracket outline with a 15-radius circle.

rationale.evidence: The Korean name means flag-hoisting bracket, and the 17 entities (16 short LINEs plus one CIRCLE r=15) trace a small 64x140 bracket profile — pure equipment depiction. Everything sits on layer 0, typical for standalone symbol blocks meant to inherit insert-layer properties.
rationale.rule: Named equipment blocks at accessory scale (tens of mm) drawn on layer 0 are unit symbols; nothing at this scale can be a wall or plan fragment.
rationale.counterfactual: Only a scale reinterpretation (units in cm/m rather than mm) could make this large enough to be building fabric, and the name would still contradict that.
rationale.uncertainty: Whether this is a plan or elevation view of the bracket is indeterminate; drawing units are assumed to be mm from context.

notes: Non-powered roof ventilator 300 symbol: centered circle r=150 with axis lines and X-cross on dedicated 무동력흡출기 layers.

rationale.evidence: 8 entities centered on the origin: a 150-radius CIRCLE with full crosshair axis lines (±325) and a diagonal X (7BE1/7BE2) on a 'CON' sublayer — the standard schematic for a 300-diameter gravity ventilator, matching the name 무동력휀300. Dedicated equipment layers (무동력흡출기/…CON) confirm an MEP symbol.
rationale.rule: Circle-plus-crosshair-plus-X compositions centered at origin on equipment-named layers are mechanical fixture symbols; symmetric axis lines are locator graphics, not geometry of the building.
rationale.counterfactual: If the crosshair lines extended far beyond the symbol and joined other fabric, or the layers were architectural, the locator-graphic reading would weaken.
rationale.uncertainty: Whether 'CON' marks a connection/센터 guide sublayer is inferred; mounting context (roof vs wall) is not recoverable from the block alone.

notes: Common washbasin/counter (세면대_공통) fixture block on FUR layer, drawn at world coordinates (~765000,-195000) with 139 splines.

rationale.evidence: The name means 'washbasin_common' and all 191 entities are on the FUR layer, with 139 SPLINEs and 16 ARCs shaping basin curves, a faucet circle (r=17.5), and a ~505x490 counter outline. Curve-heavy single-FUR-layer content is the sanitary-fixture signature.
rationale.rule: Named sanitary fixtures on FUR-type layers are furniture/fixture blocks in this taxonomy; basin geometry is drawn with splines/arcs, which walls never are.
rationale.counterfactual: If the outer rectangle lines paired at 100-200mm offsets with poche, part of the block could be wall-backed counter fabric, nudging wall_likelihood up.
rationale.uncertainty: The block's base point is far from origin (world coordinates ~765km/-196km in mm), so whether the straight boundary lines are counter edges or traced room walls cannot be settled without the parent plan.

notes: Faucet (수전) symbol on an A06-시설물-설비 (facilities/plumbing) layer: 90x108mm arc-and-circle valve/spout figure.

rationale.evidence: The name means faucet and the 33 entities — 19 ARCs, 3 concentric-scale CIRCLEs (r≈8.8/22.2/23.6), short LINEs, 2 POINTs — fit a plan/front faucet figure within a 90x108mm bbox. The layer A06-시설물-설비 explicitly declares plumbing-equipment content.
rationale.rule: Sub-100mm blocks on equipment/설비 layers with arc-dominated geometry are plumbing fixture symbols; scale alone excludes any wall or plan-fragment reading.
rationale.counterfactual: A units misread (block drawn in meters) would be the only route to reinterpreting this as large geometry, and the fixture name would still contradict it.
rationale.uncertainty: POINT entities' purpose (snap nodes vs connection markers) is not determinable; whether the view is plan or elevation of the faucet is ambiguous.

notes: Faucet variant (수전-1) on its own 수전 layer, drawn at offset world coordinates (~33550,-11100): lever-style faucet with symmetric handle lines.

rationale.evidence: Named 수전-1 with all 15 entities on a 수전 (faucet) layer; symmetric slanted line pairs (688E/688F vs 6895/6896), small CIRCLE r=5.9, ARCs and small LWPOLYLINEs compose a ~17x88mm faucet figure. Scale and name both scream fixture symbol.
rationale.rule: Suffix variant of a named fixture with fixture-scale geometry on a fixture-named layer keeps the symbol role; tiny (<100mm) extents categorically exclude building fabric.
rationale.counterfactual: Only if the layer or name were generic and the extents building-scale would any non-symbol role become plausible.
rationale.uncertainty: The block's remote base point (~33.5m offset in mm units) means its insertion behavior is odd; whether it duplicates 수전 (item 19) functionally or depicts a different faucet type cannot be resolved here.
